does: This paper introduces SCICONVBENCH, a benchmark designed to evaluate how well large language models can engage in multi-turn dialogue to clarify and refine poorly specified scientific problems before computation. The benchmark covers four computational science domains—fluid mechanics, solid mechanics, materials science, and PDEs—and focuses on two core capabilities: disambiguation (asking clarifying questions to elicit missing information) and inconsistency resolution (detecting and correcting contradictory specifications in user requests). The authors pair a structured task ontology with a rubric-based evaluation framework to systematically measure model performance across realistic scientific assistance scenarios where users initially formulate vague or internally contradictory requests.
connects: This work extends prior benchmarking efforts in scientific AI that have primarily evaluated knowledge retrieval, reasoning, and code generation in well-posed problem settings. It addresses a gap identified in existing evaluations by recognizing that practical scientific assistance requires dialogue-driven problem formulation before formal computation. The research builds on conversational AI evaluation methodologies and task-based assessment frameworks, adapting them to the specialized context of computational science where mathematical precision and physical consistency matter critically. It connects to broader work on clarification dialogues in dialogue systems and to research on how AI can help scientists formalize vague conceptual questions into executable specifications.
enables: This benchmark enables future development of LLMs specifically optimized for scientific dialogue and collaborative problem formulation rather than just solution execution. It creates a foundation for measuring whether models can reliably guide users toward well-posed problems, potentially reducing errors and wasted computation in scientific applications. The work points toward improved scientific AI assistants that function as interactive partners in problem definition, which could be particularly valuable in interdisciplinary research where domain experts must communicate across specialties. It also opens directions for studying how clarification strategies differ across scientific domains and for developing training methods that improve models' ability to elicit and validate scientific specifications.
